methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $2.0B last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Chips & Semiconductors. Range: $500M - $5.0B.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Semiconductors P/S median is 24.4x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

private.name: Etched
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series A), post-money $5B

private.name: Substrate
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series A), post-money $1B+

private.name: Rain AI
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series A), post-money $500M

data.narrative:
d-Matrix is a Series A chip design company founded by Sid Sheth, currently valued at $2.0B post-money following $450M in total fundraising. The valuation is benchmarked against a comp universe of 3 public semiconductor players (ALAB, NVDA, AMD) and 8 private frontier-chip companies, with immediate peer set including Etched, Substrate, and Rain AI.

Two triangulation methods are applicable. Stage-progression analysis applies a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical for Series A advancement, yielding $4.0B. Peer-median analysis across three same-stage, same-sector comparables produces $1.0B, with the peer set ranging from $500M (Rain AI) to $5.0B (Etched). Public-comp anchoring via P/S multiples (24.4x median across the three public tickers) is not applicable due to absence of target revenue data, standard for private companies.

The two applicable methods support a valuation range of $850M (low) to $4.6B (high), with a median of $2.5B. The current $2.0B post-money valuation sits within this range, slightly below the median. Key uncertainties include the wide dispersion in peer valuations (Etched at $5.0B vs. Rain AI at $500M suggests heterogeneous investor expectations within the cohort) and the absence of revenue or unit economics, preventing ratio-based validation. The stage-progression method carries inherent sensitivity to multiplier assumptions for early-stage semiconductor investments.
